items.q: Which component handles visual and spatial information?
items.why: Students mix up the slave systems. The phonological loop is for auditory/verbal information; the sketchpad is for visual/spatial. The central executive coordinates but doesn't store.

items.q: In Milgram's study, what percentage of participants gave the maximum 450V shock?
items.why: Students remember 'most' obeyed and assume all did. Actually 65% went to 450V; 35% defied. This nuance matters because it shows obedience is strong but not absolute.

items.q: Asch's line study primarily demonstrates which type of conformity?
items.correct: Normative social influence (compliance to fit in).
items.misconception: Informational social influence (believing others know better).
items.why: Students assume participants genuinely believed the wrong line was correct. Asch's debriefs showed they knew it was wrong but conformed publicly to avoid rejection - this is normative, not informational.

items.q: Acting on behalf of an authority is Milgram's:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Agentic state. “Autonomous state” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: An AO3 weakness of Asch is that:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: It lacks temporal validity. “It used random sampling” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Asch found roughly what percentage of participants conformed at least once?
items.why: The real figure is 75. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
